title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement C.martinez0@outlook.com. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct

title: Getting Started with nycOpenData
text: Welcome nycOpenData package, R package dedicated helping R users connect NYC Open Data Portal! nycOpenData package provides streamlined interface accessing New York City’s vast open data resources. connects directly NYC Open Data Portal, helping users bridge gap raw city APIs tidy data analysis. two ways:

title: Getting Started with nycOpenData
text: primary way pull data package nyc_pull_dataset() function, works tandem nyc_list_datasets(). workflows require users manually construct API requests manage authentication details. first step call nyc_list_datasets() see datasets list available use nyc_pull_dataset() function. provides information datasets available live NYC Open Data catalog used package. output includes columns dataset title, description, link source. important pieces key uid. need either order use nyc_pull_dataset() function. can put either key value uid value dataset = filter inside nyc_pull_dataset(). instance, want pull dataset Motor Vehicle Collisions - Crashes, can use either methods : matter put uid key value dataset =, successfully get data!
code: catalog <- nyc_list_datasets()  catalog |>   filter(grepl("collision", name, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>   select(key, uid, name) #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   key                                                                uid   name  #>   <chr>                                                              <chr> <chr> #> 1 collisions_involving_vehicles_managed_by_department_of_health_and… knr6… Coll… #> 2 motor_vehicle_collisions_vehicles                                  bm4k… Moto… #> 3 motor_vehicle_collisions_person                                    f55k… Moto… #> 4 motor_vehicle_collisions_crashes                                   h9gi… Moto… nyc_motor_vehicle_collisions_data <- nyc_pull_dataset(   dataset = "h9gi-nx95", limit = 2)  nyc_motor_vehicle_collisions_data <- nyc_pull_dataset(   dataset = "motor_vehicle_collisions_crashes", limit = 2)

title: Getting Started with nycOpenData
text: easiest workflow use nyc_list_datasets() together nyc_pull_dataset(). catalog retrieved dynamically NYC Open Data, many newly published datasets automatically available package. event particular dataset want use R list, can use nyc_any_dataset(). requirement dataset’s API endpoint (URL provided NYC Open Data Portal). NYC Open Data JSON endpoints typically follow structure: https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/<dataset_uid>.json example, Motor Vehicle Collisions dataset UID "h9gi-nx95", JSON endpoint becomes: https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/h9gi-nx95.json steps get : NYC Open Data Portal, go dataset want work . Click “Export” (next actions button right hand side). Click “API Endpoint”. Click “SODA2” “Version”. Copy API Endpoint. example use nyc_any_dataset() API endpoint discovered, pull data nyc_pull_dataset() example:

title: Getting Started with nycOpenData
text: functions provide access NYC Open Data, serve slightly different purposes. general: Use nyc_pull_dataset() dataset available nyc_list_datasets() Use nyc_any_dataset() working datasets outside catalog Together, functions allow users either quickly access datasets flexibly query dataset available NYC Open Data portal.

title: Getting Started with nycOpenData
text: NYC population almost 8.5 million people, lot people taking public transportation, still many drivers. Unfortunately, sometimes crashes take place, collision data contained dataset, found . R, nycOpenData package can used pull data directly. using nyc_pull_dataset() function, can gather recent motor vehicle collision records New York City filter based columns available dataset. Let’s take example last 2 requests borough Brooklyn. nyc_pull_dataset() function can filter based columns dataset. filter, add filters = list() put whatever filters like inside. colnames call , know column called “borough” can use accomplish . Success! calling brooklyn_collisions dataset see 2 rows data, distinct() call see borough featured dataset BROOKLYN. One strongest qualities function ability filter based multiple columns. Let’s put everything together get dataset last 50 collisions Brooklyn involving Sedan. successfully created dataset containing 50 recent Brooklyn collisions involving Sedan. Advanced users may also provide raw SoQL queries argument nyc_pull_dataset().
code: brooklyn_collisions <- nyc_pull_dataset(dataset = "h9gi-nx95",limit = 2, timeout_sec = 90, filters = list(borough = "BROOKLYN")) brooklyn_collisions #> # A tibble: 2 × 27 #>   crash_date          crash_time borough  zip_code latitude longitude #>   <dttm>              <chr>      <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 2023-11-01 00:00:00 1:29       BROOKLYN    11230     40.6     -74.0 #> 2 2021-09-11 00:00:00 9:35       BROOKLYN    11208     40.7     -73.9 #> # ℹ 21 more variables: on_street_name <chr>, off_street_name <chr>, #> #   number_of_persons_injured <dbl>, number_of_persons_killed <dbl>, #> #   number_of_pedestrians_injured <dbl>, number_of_pedestrians_killed <dbl>, #> #   number_of_cyclist_injured <dbl>, number_of_cyclist_killed <dbl>, #> #   number_of_motorist_injured <dbl>, number_of_motorist_killed <dbl>, #> #   contributing_factor_vehicle_1 <chr>, contributing_factor_vehicle_2 <chr>, #> #   contributing_factor_vehicle_3 <chr>, collision_id <dbl>, …  # Checking to see the filtering worked brooklyn_collisions |>   distinct(borough) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   borough  #>   <chr>    #> 1 BROOKLYN # Creating the dataset brooklyn_sedan <- nyc_pull_dataset("h9gi-nx95", limit = 50, timeout_sec = 90, filters = list(vehicle_type_code1 = "Sedan", borough = "BROOKLYN"))  # Calling head of our new dataset brooklyn_sedan |>   slice_head(n = 6) #> # A tibble: 6 × 29 #>   crash_date          crash_time borough  zip_code latitude longitude #>   <dttm>              <chr>      <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 2021-09-11 00:00:00 9:35       BROOKLYN    11208     40.7     -73.9 #> 2 2021-12-14 00:00:00 21:10      BROOKLYN    11207     40.7     -73.9 #> 3 2021-12-14 00:00:00 20:03      BROOKLYN    11226     40.7     -74.0 #> 4 2021-12-14 00:00:00 17:31      BROOKLYN    11230     40.6     -74.0 #> 5 2021-12-14 00:00:00 20:13      BROOKLYN    11215     40.7     -74.0 #> 6 2021-12-14 00:00:00 12:54      BROOKLYN    11217     40.7     -74.0 #> # ℹ 23 more variables: cross_street_name <chr>, #> #   number_of_persons_injured <dbl>, number_of_persons_killed <dbl>, #> #   number_of_pedestrians_injured <dbl>, number_of_pedestrians_killed <dbl>, #> #   number_of_cyclist_injured <dbl>, number_of_cyclist_killed <dbl>, #> #   number_of_motorist_injured <dbl>, number_of_motorist_killed <dbl>, #> #   contributing_factor_vehicle_1 <chr>, collision_id <dbl>, #> #   vehicle_type_code1 <chr>, contributing_factor_vehicle_2 <chr>, …  # Quick check to make sure our filtering worked brooklyn_sedan |>   summarize(rows = n()) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>    rows #>   <int> #> 1    50  brooklyn_sedan |>   distinct(vehicle_type_code1) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   vehicle_type_code1 #>   <chr>              #> 1 Sedan  brooklyn_sedan |>   distinct(borough) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   borough  #>   <chr>    #> 1 BROOKLYN

title: Getting Started with nycOpenData
text: Now successfully pulled data R, let’s mini analysis using contributing_factor_vehicle_1 column, figure main reasons collisions. , create bar graph contributing factors associated collisions. Bar chart showing frequency collision contributing factors Brooklyn involving Sedan. graph shows us contributing factors appeared frequently, also often factor occurred dataset.
code: # Visualizing the distribution, ordered by frequency brooklyn_sedan |>   count(contributing_factor_vehicle_1) |>   ggplot(aes(     x = n,     y = reorder(contributing_factor_vehicle_1, n)   )) +   geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +   theme_minimal() +   labs(     title = "Top 50 Collisions in Brooklyn Involving a Sedan",     x = "Number of Collisions",     y = "Contributing Factor"   )

what: A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
title: A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
text: nycOpenData provides lightweight R interface NYC Open Data Socrata API. package allows users search, filter, download datasets NYC Open Data Portal directly R without manually constructing API queries, handling JSON responses, performing type conversion. Designed primarily students, educators, researchers, nycOpenData reduces technical overhead required begin working civic datasets still exposing underlying structure NYC Open Data ecosystem. Version 0.2.2 introduces streamlined, catalog-driven interface NYC Open Data. users may still explore datasets NYC Open Data Portal , nycOpenData streamlines transition discovery reproducible analysis within R workflows.

title: A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
text: package wraps NYC Open Data Portal’s Socrata API. Internally, nycOpenData: retrieves metadata live NYC Open Data catalog tables constructs parameterized HTTP GET requests downloads JSON responses Socrata endpoints converts results tidy tibble outputs optionally performs automatic column type coercion Automatic type coercion uses heuristic-based parsing infer common column types Socrata API responses. workflows begin nyc_list_datasets(), retrieves live catalog available datasets NYC Open Data (5tqd-u88y). Datasets can downloaded using either: human-readable catalog key (recommended) raw Socrata dataset UID (e.g. "erm2-nwe9") catalog key designed easier remember use classroom settings, Socrata UID stable identifier used internally NYC Open Data Portal. package provides three core functions: nyc_list_datasets() — Retrieve live catalog available NYC Open Data datasets, including dataset titles, human-readable keys, Socrata UIDs, endpoint URLs, metadata used throughout package. nyc_pull_dataset() — Download cataloged NYC Open Data datasets using either human-readable key dataset UID, support filtering, ordering, date ranges, automatic type coercion, optional column name cleaning. nyc_any_dataset() — Pull data directly arbitrary NYC Open Data Socrata JSON endpoints without requiring inclusion internal package catalog. Datasets pulled via nyc_pull_dataset() automatically apply sensible defaults catalog (default ordering date fields), still allowing user control : limit filters date / / order clean_names coerce_types Datasets can referenced using either: human-readable catalog key (recommended), underlying Socrata dataset UID (e.g. "erm2-nwe9") catalog key system designed improve readability usability classroom reproducible research settings, memorizing opaque Socrata UIDs can create unnecessary friction new users. functions return clean tibble outputs support filtering viafilters = list(field = "value"). Advanced users may optionally provide raw SoQL queries argument. SoQL (Socrata Query Language) filtering query syntax used Socrata-powered open data portals: https://dev.socrata.com/docs/queries/

title: A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
text: filters argument accepts named lists automatically generates appropriate SoQL filtering statements. example:
code: library(nycOpenData) library(dplyr) ## Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.5.2  ##  ## Attaching package: 'dplyr'  ## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': ##  ##     filter, lag  ## The following objects are masked from 'package:base': ##  ##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union # Browse available datasets catalog <- nyc_list_datasets()  # Search for 311-related datasets catalog %>%   filter(grepl("311", name, ignore.case = TRUE)) %>%   select(key, name) ## # A tibble: 15 × 2 ##    key                                            name                           ##    <chr>                                          <chr>                          ##  1 x311_service_requests_for_2004                 311 Service Requests for 2004  ##  2 x311_call_center_inquiry                       311 Call Center Inquiry        ##  3 x311_service_level_agreements                  311 Service Level Agreements   ##  4 x311_service_requests_for_2008                 311 Service Requests for 2008  ##  5 x311_interpreter_wait_time                     311 Interpreter Wait Time      ##  6 x311_service_requests_for_2009                 311 Service Requests for 2009  ##  7 x311_service_requests_from_2010_to_2019        311 Service Requests from 201… ##  8 x311_service_requests_for_2007                 311 Service Requests for 2007  ##  9 x311_service_requests_for_2005                 311 Service Requests for 2005  ## 10 x311_service_requests_from_2020_to_present     311 Service Requests from 202… ## 11 x311_service_requests_for_2006                 311 Service Requests for 2006  ## 12 public_feedback_on_311_request_complaint_types Public feedback on 311 reques… ## 13 x311_resolution_satisfaction_survey            311 Resolution Satisfaction S… ## 14 x311_web_content_services                      311 Web Content - Services     ## 15 x311_customer_satisfaction_survey              311 Customer Satisfaction Sur… # Pull recent 311 requests requests <- nyc_pull_dataset(   dataset = "x311_service_requests_from_2020_to_present",   limit = 100 )  # Pull filtered data brooklyn_nypd <- nyc_pull_dataset(   dataset = "x311_service_requests_from_2020_to_present",   limit = 100,   filters = list(     agency = "NYPD",     city = "BROOKLYN"   ) ) filters = list(   borough = c("BROOKLYN", "QUEENS") )

title: A Lightweight Interface to NYC Open Data APIs
text: RSocrata package provides general interface Socrata-backed portal, nycOpenData specifically tailored New York City ecosystem. Low-friction onboarding: Designed students can begin working NYC civic datasets minutes without needing manually construct API requests. Readable dataset identifiers: Uses stable catalog-driven keys instead requiring users memorize Socrata dataset UIDs. Pedagogical design: Emphasizes transparency, reproducibility, classroom-friendly workflows teaching open data literacy. Lightweight workflow: Returns clean tibble outputs minimal configuration dependencies.

what: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
title: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
text: Downloads data NYC Open Data Socrata JSON endpoint returns result tibble. function useful datasets included curated catalog returned [nyc_list_datasets()].

title: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
code: nyc_any_dataset(   json_link,   limit = 10000,   timeout_sec = 30,   clean_names = TRUE,   coerce_types = TRUE )

title: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
text: json_link single Socrata dataset JSON endpoint URL, `"https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.json"`. limit Number rows retrieve. Defaults 10,000. timeout_sec Request timeout seconds. Defaults 30. clean_names Logical. `TRUE`, column names converted snake_case using [janitor::clean_names()]. Defaults `TRUE`. coerce_types Logical. `TRUE`, package attempts lightweight, heuristic-based type coercion downloading data. Columns converted least 95 percent non-missing values can parsed target type. helps avoid unsafe conversions source data inconsistent.

title: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
text: tibble containing rows requested NYC Open Data endpoint.

title: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
text: NYC Open Data datasets Socrata JSON endpoints usually follow pattern: `https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/<dataset_uid>.json` example, 311 Service Requests dataset Socrata UID `"erm2-nwe9"`, JSON endpoint : `https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.json` Users can find dataset's UID NYC Open Data Portal URL, API documentation page dataset, output [nyc_list_datasets()]. `nyc_any_dataset()` bypasses package catalog sends request directly supplied JSON endpoint. Unlike [nyc_pull_dataset()], look defaults catalog keys, date fields, default ordering. function intended direct endpoint access. catalog-based workflows using readable keys Socrata UIDs, use [nyc_pull_dataset()].

title: Pull any NYC Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint — nyc_any_dataset
code: # Examples that hit the live NYC Open Data API are guarded so CRAN checks # do not fail when the network is unavailable or slow. if (interactive() && curl::has_internet()) {   # Build a JSON endpoint from a Socrata UID   uid <- "erm2-nwe9"   endpoint <- paste0("https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/", uid, ".json")    out <- try(nyc_any_dataset(endpoint, limit = 3), silent = TRUE)   if (!inherits(out, "try-error")) {     head(out)   } }

text: returned tibble includes official Socrata dataset `uid` human-readable `key`. `uid` stable identifier used NYC Open Data Portal Socrata API, `key` generated dataset name using [janitor::make_clean_names()] make datasets easier reference R code, especially classroom reproducible research settings. users begin calling `nyc_list_datasets()`, searching returned catalog dataset interest, passing either `key` `uid` [nyc_pull_dataset()].

code: if (interactive() && curl::has_internet()) {   catalog <- nyc_list_datasets()    # View available datasets   catalog    # Search for 311-related datasets   catalog[grepl("311", catalog$name, ignore.case = TRUE), c("key", "uid", "name")] }

title: Pull a NYC Open Data dataset — nyc_pull_dataset
code: nyc_pull_dataset(   dataset,   limit = 10000,   filters = list(),   date = NULL,   from = NULL,   to = NULL,   date_field = NULL,   where = NULL,   order = NULL,   timeout_sec = 30,   clean_names = TRUE,   coerce_types = TRUE )

title: Pull a NYC Open Data dataset — nyc_pull_dataset
text: dataset single dataset `key` Socrata dataset `uid` [nyc_list_datasets()]. example, key may look like `"x311_service_requests_from_2020_to_present"`, UID may look like `"erm2-nwe9"`. limit Number rows retrieve. Defaults 10,000. filters Optional named list exact-match filters. list name field name dataset, value value values match. Vector values translated SQL-style `` conditions generated SoQL query. example, `filters = list(borough = c("BROOKLYN", "QUEENS"))` returns rows `borough` either `"BROOKLYN"` `"QUEENS"`. date Optional single date used match records day. Requires `date_field`. Optional start date, inclusive. Requires `date_field`. Optional end date, exclusive. Requires `date_field`. date_field Optional date datetime column use `date`, ``, ``. must supplied date filter used. Users can identify available date columns inspecting dataset NYC Open Data Portal pulling small sample `limit`. Optional raw SoQL `` clause advanced filtering. SoQL Socrata Query Language used NYC Open Data. `date`, ``, `` also supplied, generated conditions combined `` using ``. order Optional raw SoQL `ORDER ` clause, `"created_date DESC"`. timeout_sec Request timeout seconds. Defaults 30. clean_names Logical. `TRUE`, column names converted snake_case using [janitor::clean_names()]. Defaults `TRUE`. coerce_types Logical. `TRUE`, package attempts lightweight, heuristic-based type coercion downloading data. Columns converted least 95 percent non-missing values can parsed target type. helps avoid unsafe conversions source data inconsistent.

title: Pull a NYC Open Data dataset — nyc_pull_dataset
text: tibble containing rows requested NYC Open Data dataset.

title: Pull a NYC Open Data dataset — nyc_pull_dataset
text: catalog `key` supplied, `nyc_pull_dataset()` first retrieves live NYC Open Data catalog look corresponding Socrata `uid`, sends second request download dataset . Supplying `uid` directly stable avoids ambiguity, keys provided readability classroom-friendly workflows. Dataset keys generated dataset names using [janitor::make_clean_names()]. keys derived live catalog metadata, Socrata UIDs stable identifiers. `nyc_pull_dataset()` designed common catalog-based workflows. arbitrary Socrata JSON endpoints included package catalog, use [nyc_any_dataset()]. `filters` argument intended simple exact-match filtering. complex conditions, use `` argument raw SoQL syntax. Internally, filter field names wrapped `TRIM()` constructing SoQL queries reduce mismatches caused leading trailing whitespace source data. Type coercion intentionally conservative. `coerce_types = TRUE`, package attempts infer common R column types API response, columns inconsistent values may remain character columns. Datetime coercion also conservative. Timezone offsets sub-second precision may always preserved automatic parsing, columns inconsistent datetime formats may remain character columns.

title: Pull a NYC Open Data dataset — nyc_pull_dataset
code: if (interactive() && curl::has_internet()) {   # Pull by human-readable key   nyc_pull_dataset("x311_service_requests_from_2020_to_present", limit = 3)    # Pull by Socrata UID   nyc_pull_dataset("erm2-nwe9", limit = 3)    # Filter to one borough   nyc_pull_dataset(     "erm2-nwe9",     limit = 3,     filters = list(borough = "QUEENS")   )    # Filter to multiple boroughs   nyc_pull_dataset(     "erm2-nwe9",     limit = 10,     filters = list(borough = c("BROOKLYN", "QUEENS"))   )    # Date filtering   nyc_pull_dataset(     "erm2-nwe9",     from = "2023-01-01",     to = "2024-01-01",     date_field = "created_date",     limit = 100   )    # Advanced filtering with raw SoQL   nyc_pull_dataset(     "erm2-nwe9",     where = "borough = 'BROOKLYN' AND agency = 'NYPD'",     order = "created_date DESC",     limit = 100   ) }

title: nycOpenData 0.2.2
text: release continues transition catalog-driven workflow accessing NYC Open Data datasets R, emphasizing transparency, reproducibility, streamlined API access. nyc_list_datasets() now serves primary entry point discovering datasets available live NYC Open Data catalog. nyc_pull_dataset() provides unified interface downloading datasets using either human-readable catalog keys Socrata dataset UIDs. Dataset keys generated dynamically using janitor::make_clean_names() based live catalog metadata. Expanded support flexible dataset discovery retrieval without requiring dedicated dataset wrapper functions.

title: nycOpenData 0.2.2
text: human-readable catalog keys (e.g., "motor_vehicle_collisions_crashes") stable Socrata dataset UIDs (e.g., "h9gi-nx95") Added support date filtering using date, , arguments user-specified date_field. Improved filtering support vectorized filters translated SQL-style clauses. Improved support direct Socrata endpoint access nyc_any_dataset().

title: nycOpenData 0.2.2
text: package architecture Socrata API workflows SoQL usage dataset keys versus UIDs catalog-driven dataset retrieval type coercion behavior nyc_list_datasets() nyc_pull_dataset() nyc_any_dataset() realistic catalog search workflows filtering date-based queries direct endpoint access Revised vignettes align new catalog-driven architecture remove outdated wrapper-function references. Added additional development testing documentation vcr webmockr.

title: nycOpenData 0.2.2
text: Refactored internal request handling support dynamic catalog lookup. Improved validation dataset identifiers, filters, date-filtering parameters. Improved handling conservative heuristic-based type coercion. Reduced reliance dedicated dataset wrapper functions favor unified interface.

title: nycOpenData 0.2.2
text: Updated package citation information version consistency across package documentation. Continued improvements API reliability, documentation clarity, internal utilities.
